serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of wattalapam weighs approximately 100g and provides around 280 kcal, making it a moderate-calorie dessert portion.

nutrient_source.title: Sri Lanka Food Composition Tables — Medical Research Institute (MRI) 2012 + USDA SR Legacy component calculation (coconut milk, kithul jaggery, egg)
nutrient_source.organization: Medical Research Institute, Ministry of Health, Sri Lanka; USDA Agricultural Research Service (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Wattalapam nutrient values derived from Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Tables (2012) as primary regional source, supplemented by USDA SR Legacy component-level data for coconut milk, egg, and kithul jaggery. No single approved-source entry exists for the complete dish; values represent a weighted component calculation consistent with standard recipe proportions. Confidence: medium-high.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI for Wattalapam — analogous estimate based on kithul jaggery GI (~38, University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed literature) moderated by coconut milk fat (14g) and egg protein content; whole-dish GI estimated at ~55
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate basis); peer-reviewed GI literature for palm jaggery
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Wattalapam exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~55 based on: (1) kithul palm jaggery GI ~38 (lower than refined sucrose GI ~65, per published literature on palm sugars); (2) fat-protein moderation effect of coconut milk (~14g fat per 100g) and egg protein (~4.5g per 100g), which slow gastric emptying and reduce postprandial glucose response; (3) analogy with similar steamed egg-and-coconut-milk custard desserts. The University of Sydney GI Database classifies GI 55 as the upper boundary of the low category (low: 55 or below). GL per 100g serving = GI x available carbohydrate / 100 = 55 x 22 / 100 = 12.1. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~55mg per 100g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Wattalapam contains approximately 55mg sodium per 100g piece, which is very low and poses no meaningful sodium concern for individuals managing hypertension; however, the high saturated fat content (10g per serving) warrants attention for overall cardiovascular risk management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~55 (low boundary), GL ~12.1, and high total sugar content (22g per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Wattalapam has a GI of approximately 55 (at the upper boundary of the low category) and a glycemic load of 12.1 per 100g piece; while kithul palm jaggery has a lower GI (~38) than refined sugar (~65), the 22g total sugar and 280 kcal per serving mean this dessert is not suitable for regular consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and should be treated as an occasional indulgence with strict portion control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (~10g per 100g, predominantly lauric and myristic acid from coconut milk) and moderate cholesterol (~100mg from eggs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 100g serving of wattalapam provides approximately 10g saturated fat (predominantly from coconut milk) and 100mg cholesterol from eggs; individuals with cardiovascular disease or elevated LDL cholesterol should limit consumption, as regular intake of high saturated fat foods is associated with adverse lipid profiles.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat and moderate sugar load may promote systemic inflammation with regular consumption
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high saturated fat (~10g per serving from coconut milk) and moderate sugar content (~22g per serving) in wattalapam may contribute to pro-inflammatory dietary patterns if consumed regularly by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; occasional consumption is unlikely to be harmful.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wattalapam is a traditional Sri Lankan steamed custard pudding made from coconut milk, eggs, and kithul palm jaggery — a dark, aromatic treacle derived from the Caryota urens (fishtail palm) native to Sri Lanka. The dish is spiced with cardamom, cloves, and sometimes vanilla or nutmeg, giving it a distinctive warm, caramel-like flavour with a silky, dense custard texture. It is believed to have been introduced to Sri Lanka by Malay soldiers and settlers brought by the Dutch East India Company in the 17th–18th centuries, and shares heritage with Malay kuih talam and Philippine leche flan — all reflecting shared custard pudding traditions across the Muslim maritime trading world. The uniquely Sri Lankan element is kithul jaggery, which has a lower glycemic index (~38) compared to refined sugar (~65), imparting a complex, slightly smoky sweetness. Per 100g serving: approximately 280 kcal, 4.5g protein, 14g fat (predominantly saturated from coconut milk — lauric and myristic acid), 34g carbohydrate, 22g sugar, 0.5g dietary fibre, 55mg sodium, and 100mg cholesterol from eggs. GI is estimated at ~55 (low category per University of Sydney GI Database boundary) and glycemic load ~12.1 per piece. Despite kithul jaggery's lower GI advantage, wattalapam remains a moderate-concern dessert for Type 2 Diabetes due to its total sugar and calorie load. It is gluten-free, dairy-free, vegetarian, and halal. Available at Sri Lankan restaurants and festive/wedding catering in Singapore and Sri Lanka.
meal_context: Sri Lankan festive and wedding food

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Tables (2012) as primary regional source supplemented by USDA SR Legacy component-level calculation for coconut milk, egg, and kithul jaggery. No single approved-source entry covers the complete dish. GI is an analogous estimate with documented basis. Overall confidence is medium (not high, as no exact dish-level entry exists in a single approved source).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for wattalapam not available in Sri Lanka MRI FCP (2012) or USDA SR Legacy at the dish level. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the complete steamed dish not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy component data for coconut milk and egg — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation. vitamin_c_mg set to 0.0: wattalapam contains no vitamin C-contributing ingredients; confirmed negligible per component analysis. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate based on kithul jaggery GI (~38) moderated by coconut milk fat and egg protein; no direct GI measurement for wattalapam exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (low: 55 or below); gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Wattalapam is not a diabetes-safe dessert despite kithul jaggery's lower GI advantage — the 22g total sugar and 280 kcal per serving make it a moderate-concern food for T2DM. The dish is believed to have Malay-Sri Lankan hybrid heritage introduced via the Dutch East India Company's Malay Regiment in the 17th–18th centuries. Kithul jaggery (from Caryota urens) is the uniquely Sri Lankan ingredient distinguishing this from all other regional custard puddings. Source: Sri Lanka MRI FCP (2012) + USDA SR Legacy component calculation.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low'. Per the University of Sydney GI Database official classification, GI 55 is the upper boundary of the low category (low = GI 55 or below; medium = GI 56–69; high = GI 70+). The previous iteration incorrectly used 'medium' for GI 55. This was the sole failing criterion (q4) identified by the validator in iteration 2.
Updated gi_source.source_match_notes to explicitly document that GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category per University of Sydney GI Database classification.
Updated health_context.type2_diabetes.key_factor to reflect 'low boundary' GI classification for accuracy.
Updated notes field to document the GI category classification rationale.
Updated natural_language_description to reflect 'low category per University of Sydney GI Database boundary' for GI 55.
All other sections preserved from iteration 2 which passed all other gates and criteria.
